title: Hello dolly bars
subtitle: The magic cookie bar — seven layers in one pan
excerpt: Biscuit base, sweetened condensed milk, desiccated coconut, chocolate chips, butterscotch chips, chopped nuts, and then the oven does the rest. The bars set as they cool to a rich, fudgy slab that cuts into clean squares.
sourceNotes: Hello dolly bars (also known as magic cookie bars or seven-layer bars) are an American confection documented from the mid-twentieth century, popularised on condensed milk can labels and in church and school recipe pamphlets that entered the public domain. The recipe requires no mixing: layers are simply pressed or scattered into the pan, which accounts for its widespread adoption.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut and freeze in layers separated by baking paper. Keeps for 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ideal to make the day before — the bars firm up overnight in the fridge and cut cleanly.

glossaryTerms.definition: Canned cow's milk with most of the water removed and a large quantity of sugar added. It caramelises as it bakes, binding the layers together without any mixing required.

content.text: No mixer needed, no creaming, no melting. This recipe is layer and bake. The
is poured over the layers and baked until it has set to a caramel-coloured fudge that holds everything together. The result is intensely sweet and satisfying in small squares.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional (155°C fan). Line a 30 × 20 cm tin with baking paper, leaving an overhang on both sides to lift the bars out later. Pour the melted butter into the tin and tilt to coat the base. Scatter the biscuit crumbs evenly over the butter and press gently to form a compact base. Pour the condensed milk evenly over the biscuit layer — it should cover it completely. Scatter the coconut, then the chocolate chips, then the butterscotch chips, then the walnuts, pressing each layer gently into the one below.

Bake for 28–32 minutes until the coconut and edges are golden brown and the condensed milk has set to a caramel-coloured layer. The surface should look set and slightly bubbled. Cool completely in the tin, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours before lifting out using the paper overhang. Cut into bars with a sharp knife. The colder they are, the cleaner the cuts.
